transcript: Welcome to Lesson 6.4 Email Design Best Practices. Over the past three lessons, you have built a comprehensive foundation. You understand email deliverability, the four campaign types, and how to write copy that drives, opens, and clicks. Now we address the final layer before you build your first campaign in Go High Level Design. Email design is not about making things look beautiful. It is about making your copy easier to read, easier to act on, and more credible to trust. A well-designed email is invisible. The reader absorbs the message without noticing the structure that delivered it. A poorly designed email is painfully visible. The reader struggles to find the headline, cannot tap the CTA button on their phone, and closes the email before reaching the offer. By the end of this lesson, you will have the layout rules, branding principles, mobile-first guidelines, and a complete pre-sent checklist that ensures every email you build in GoHighLevel delivers your copy at full strength. Let's get into it. Before we get into specific rules, you need to internalize the hierarchy that governs every design decision you will make in email marketing. At the top of the hierarchy is copy. The words, subject line, body, CTA are the campaign. They are what persuades. Everything else exists to support them. In the middle is design. Design makes copy readable, credible, and easy to act on. It creates the visual environment that allows your words to be received clearly. It is not decoration. It is structure in service of communication. At the base is delivery infrastructure. Your platform, your automation, your sending domain. When this layer works correctly, it is invisible. When it breaks, nothing else matters. Here is the failure mode you need to avoid. When design competes with copy. When decorative images draw the eye away from the headline. When multiple font sizes and colors create visual noise. When the CTA button is buried under competing elements. The reader admires the presentation and then closes the email without acting. The design won the attention contest. The copy lost the conversion. When design serves copy, clean layout guiding the eye directly to the headline, consistent typography making body text effortlessly scannable, a visually dominant CTA that is impossible to miss, the reader moves through the email naturally and takes the action the copy leads them to. Here is the principle you take from this lesson. When someone reads your email, they should remember what you said, not how it looked. Memorable design in email is invisible design. Let me walk you through the six layout fundamentals that govern every high-converting email. Rule number one, single-column layout. Use one column for all marketing emails without exception. Multi-column layouts, two or three columns side by side, look organized on a desktop but collapse into a broken unreadable stack on mobile devices. Since over 60% of your audience reads on a phone, a layout that breaks on mobile is a layout that fails for the majority of your list. Rule number two, 600 pixels maximum width. This is the industry standard for email width and has been for over a decade. Email clients like Gmail, Outlook, and Apple Mail are designed to display 600-pixel-wide emails correctly. Wider emails get cropped on many clients or require horizontal scrolling, both of which destroy the reading experience before your reader reaches a single line of copy. Rule number three, headline first, always. The first thing a reader sees after opening your email must be your headline, not your logo, not a decorative banner image. Your headline, a small logo in the header above the headline, is acceptable as a brand identifier, but the reader's first text experience must be the compelling hook you wrote in your email copywriting template. Rule number four, font sizes that respect the reader. Use a minimum of 14 pixels for body text and 18 to 22 pixels for headlines. Anything smaller forces mobile readers to zoom in, and most will not. They will simply close the email. Larger text signals confidence and readability. Rule number five. White space is not wasted space. Tadding between sections, a minimum of 16 to 24 pixels, makes emails scannable rather than overwhelming. Cramped, dense emails feel untrustworthy and are harder to read. White space signals quality and respect for the reader's time. Rule number six, a legal footer on every email. Every email you send must include a physical mailing address your business name and a clearly functional unsubscribe link These are legal requirements under CAN GDPR and most regional email regulations GoHighLevel handles this automatically when you configure your footer in the email settings but verify it is present before every send. Single column, 600 pixels wide. Headline first, readable font sizes. Generous white space. Legal footer. These six rules govern 90% of all layout decisions you will make in email design. Branding consistency in email is not about being visually impressive. It is about being reliably recognizable. When your subscriber opens an email from you, they should know within two seconds who it is from, before they even read a word. That instant recognition is built through four consistent brand elements applied to every single email in every single sequence. The first is your logo. Place it in the header of every email, top center or top left, using the same version every time. Keep it at a maximum height of 100 to 120 pixels. A logo that towers over the email header, crowds the top of the email, and delays the reader from reaching your headline. Always link your logo to the client's website. The second is typography. Use a maximum of two font families per email, one for headlines and one for body text. For body text specifically, use web-safe fonts that render reliably across all email clients. Arial, Georgia, and Verdana are the gold standard. Custom fonts can be used for headlines when rendered as images, but never for body text. If the custom font fails to load, body text in a non-websafe font will default to an ugly system font and break your layout. The third is color. One primary brand color for CTA buttons and accent elements. Never more than three colors in a single email. Always maintain high contrast between text and background. Dark text on a light background is the standard. One important technical note. Avoid using red text in your email body. Red text is commonly associated with spam by certain email client filters and can affect deliverability. The fourth is tone and voice. This is the most overlooked dimension of email branding. If your brand is professional and approachable, every email from that brand must feel professional and approachable. From the welcome sequence to the re-engagement campaign. Tone inconsistency confuses readers who have built expectations based on previous emails. Define three tone descriptors for every client and check every email against them before sending. Mobile-first design is not a preference, it is a baseline requirement. Over 60% of all email opens happen on mobile devices. This means designing for desktop first and then hoping it works on mobile is designing for failure in the majority of cases. The correct workflow is the reverse. Design for mobile first, then verify it also looks correct on desktop. Here are the six mobile-first rules that govern every email you build in GoHighLevel. Rule number one, font sizes. Body text at a minimum of 14 pixels. Headlines at a minimum of 22 pixels. On a 375 pixel wide phone screen, anything smaller forces the reader to pinch and zoom. Most readers will not. They will close the email instead. Rule number two, line length. Keep your lines to a maximum of 60 characters on mobile. Longer lines require the reader to scan horizontally across the full width of a small screen, which causes them to lose their place mid-sentence. Shorter lines create a natural vertical reading rhythm that is effortless on mobile. Rule number three, CTA button width. Your call to action button must be full width on mobile screens. A narrow centered button on a phone requires the reader to tap an accurately small target with their thumb. A full-width button spans the entire readable area and can be tapped anywhere across its surface, removing all tapping friction. Rule number four, single images only. Never use side-by-side images in an email. On mobile, multi-column image layouts stack vertically, which creates a completely different visual hierarchy from the one you designed on desktop. Single, full-width images maintain their intended relationship to the surrounding copy on every screen size. Rule number five, tap targets. Any clickable element, including text links within the body copy, must be at least 44 pixels tall to be comfortably tappable. Links embedded in body text are nearly impossible to tap accurately on a phone without accidentally tapping surrounding text buttons for your primary CTEs and minimize inline text links Rule number six always preview on mobile before sending Go High Level's email editor has a mobile preview toggle in the toolbar. Use it for every single email before scheduling. What you see in the desktop editor is not always what your mobile recipient sees. The preview takes approximately 30 seconds to check. There is no acceptable reason to skip it. Images in email are powerful when used correctly and damaging when used carelessly. Let me give you both sides clearly. Images add genuine value in four specific scenarios. A product or lead magnet mockup makes a digital asset feel tangible and valuable. The same principle you apply to landing pages in Module 4 applies directly here. here. An author or team headshot beside the sender's sign-off humanizes the brand and builds personal connection. A results screenshot or before and after comparison image makes a clean outcome visually credible. And simple branded graphic dividers between email sections improve scannability without adding significant image load risk. Now for the technical rules that determine whether your images help or hurt deliverability. The text-to-image ratio rule is the most important. Your email must be a minimum of 60% text and a maximum of 40% images. Emails that are primarily visual content, especially those that consist of a single large image, are flagged by spam filters before they ever reach an inbox. Spam filters need readable text to evaluate your content. Images provide none. Compress all images below 200 kilobytes before uploading to go high level. Large image files slow email load times. On mobile connections, a slow loading email is an email that gets closed before the CTA is ever seen. Every image must have descriptive alt text. When images are blocked, which happens by default in many email plans including Outlook, The alt text is what the reader sees in place of the image. Without alt text, the reader sees an empty white square with no context. With alt text, they see a brief description that maintains the intent of the image. Images must be hosted via URL, never attached to an email. Attached images are flagged as suspicious by virtually every spam filter. Go High Level handles this correctly. when you upload images directly in the email editor. All images are hosted on GHL servers and inserted as URLs automatically. And one absolute rule, never send an email that consists entirely of a single large image with no text. These emails have zero deliverability, failed accessibility standards, and communicate nothing to readers whose images are blocked. This checklist is your quality assurance ritual before every email leaves your hands. It takes under 5 minutes to complete and prevents the design failures that silently undermine campaigns that have strong copy and the right strategy. For layout and structure, verify that you are using a single column layout throughout and that your email width is 600 pixels or less. Confirm the headline appears first in the email body and that body text is 14 pixels or larger and headlines are 18 pixels or larger. Check that adequate white space exists between all sections and that your legal footer, physical address, business name, unsubscribe link is present and functional. For branding, confirm the logo is present in the header and matches previous emails. Verify that brand colors are used consistently and that no more than three colors appear in the email. Check that the font family is consistent with your brand guidelines and that the tone matches the three brand voice descriptors. For mobile verification, open the mobile preview toggle in Go High Level and check that the headline is readable at mobile zoom level. Verify the CTA button is full width on mobile. Confirm there are no side-by-side image columns in the mobile view. Check all tap targets are at least 44 pixels tall. Verify body text paragraphs are a maximum of 3 lines each. For images, confirm the text-to-image ratio is at least 60% text. Check all images are below 200 kilobytes. Verify all images have descriptive alt text. Confirm no section consists entirely of an image without accompanying text. Verify all images are hosted. No attachments. For the final send check, send a test email to your own inbox and open it on both your phone and your laptop. Click every link and verify all destinations are correct. Click the unsubscribe link and confirm it functions. Only then schedule 25 checkboxes. boxes Everyone matters A single broken unsubscribe link or an all email can damage your sender reputation with a single send Let me close with seven design mistakes that undermine even the best email copy and the specific fix for each one. Mistake number one, using a multi-column layout. It looks organized on desktop and completely breaks on mobile. Fix single column always without exception. Mistake number two. Leading with a large decorative banner image instead of the headline. The reader opens the email and sees a graphic, not your compelling hook. Fix, headline appears first in the email body every time. A small logo in the header is acceptable, but the first text the reader encounters must be your headline. Mistake number three. Using three or more font families or too many size variations. The visual noise competes with the content and makes the email feel unprofessional. Fix. Two font families maximum, two to three size variations maximum, applied consistently throughout. Mistake number four, a CTA button with insufficient visual contrast. If the button blends into the surrounding design, it does not get clicked, even when the copy is compelling. Fix. The CTA button must be visually dominant in its section. Adequate contrast, sufficient size, and white space surrounding it. Mistake number five. Wall of text body copy with no paragraph breaks. Long unbroken text blocks are abandoned on mobile because scanning is impossible. Fix. Maximum three lines per paragraph on mobile, consistent line breaks between thoughts and white space between all major sections. Mistake number six, missing alt text on images. When images are blocked, and they often are, the email layout collapses into empty white squares with no context. Fixed. Every image gets a short descriptive alt text label written before the email is built in GoHighLevel. Mistake number seven, skipping the test send. What you see in the email builder is not always what your recipient sees. Fonts render differently across email clients. Images load differently on different devices. Fix Always send a test email to yourself. Open it on your phone, check it on your laptop, and only then schedule the send. Fixing just the first, second, and seventh mistakes. Single column, headline first, always test eliminates the most common design failures in go-high-level email campaigns immediately. Let's lock in what you learned in this lesson. Design serves copy. Its job is to make your message easier to receive and more credible to trust. When design competes with copy for attention, conversions drop. Invisible design is the goal. Email layout fundamentals are single column structure, 600 pixels maximum width, headline appearing first, 14 to 16 pixel minimum body font, generous white space between all sections, and a legal footer on every email. Branding consistency across logo, typography, color, and tone makes every email instantly recognizable. Consistency builds reader expectations, and expectations build trust. Mobile-first design requires 14-pixel minimum body text, 22-pixel minimum headlines, full-width CTA buttons, single images only, and always previewing on mobile before sending. 60% of your audience reads on a phone. Design for them first. Images in email follow a 60% text minimum rule, must be compressed below 200 kilobytes, must have all text on every image, and must never constitute the entire email. Image-only emails are spam-filtered and inaccessible. Run the 25-point pre-send design checklist before every email, covering layout, branding, mobile images, and the final send check. It takes 5 minutes and prevents the design failures that silently undermine campaigns with great copy. Coming up next is Lesson 6.5 Building Email Campaigns in GHL You now have the complete framework for email campaigns. You understand deliverability, the four campaign types, how to write copy for each, and how to design every email for maximum performance and credibility. Lesson 6.5 is the hands-on build session where you open GoHighLevel and construct a real email campaign. Building sequences, configuring automation triggers, uploading copy from your email copywriting template, and publishing your first campaign from scratch inside the platform. Everything from Lesson 6.1 through 6.4 comes to life in this build session. Strategy becomes execution. Let's continue to Lesson 6.5. Thank you.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a comprehensive guide to email design best practices, focusing on layout, branding, and mobile-first design. The title, "Email Design Best Practices," is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a brief description that emphasizes the importance of effective copy presentation.

**Key Points:**

* **Module and Lesson Information**
	+ Module: 6
	+ Lesson: 6.4
* **Title and Description**
	+ Title: Email Design Best Practices
	+ Description: Layout, branding, and mobile-first design - how to present your copy so it lands with maximum impact
* **What You'll Learn**
	+ Why design serves copy and what happens when it competes with it
	+ The email layout fundamentals that make every email scannable and easy to act on
	+ How to build branding consistency that makes your emails instantly recognizable
	+ The mobile-first design rules that govern how 60 percent of your audience reads your emails
	+ How to use images correctly in email without triggering spam filters or slowing load times
	+ The pre-send design checklist that catches every critical error before your email goes live
* **Visual Aids**
	+ A graphic illustrating the transition from a desktop to a mobile device, highlighting the importance of mobile-first design
	+ Three icons representing layout grid, branding, and mobile first
* **Estimated Time**
	+ Estimated time: 14 minutes

In summary, the image provides a concise overview of the key takeaways from a lesson on email design best practices, emphasizing the importance of effective layout, branding, and mobile-first design in presenting copy with maximum impact.

The image presents a list of seven common email design mistakes that can undermine the effectiveness of an email campaign, along with suggested corrections. The title, "7 Email Design Mistakes That Undermine Great Copy," is prominently displayed at the top.

*   **Using a multi-column layout that breaks on mobile**
    *   Mistake: Using a multi-column layout that breaks on mobile
    *   Correction: Single column always - for every marketing email.
*   **Leading with large decorative banner image not headline**
    *   Mistake: Leading with large decorative banner image not headline
    *   Correction: Headline appears first in body - first text must be headline.
*   **Using too many fonts or font sizes (3+ creates visual noise)**
    *   Mistake: Using too many fonts or font sizes (3+ creates visual noise)
    *   Correction: Two font families maximum, 2-3 size variations total.
*   **CTA button same visual weight as surrounding elements**
    *   Mistake: CTA button same visual weight as surrounding elements
    *   Correction: CTA must be visually dominant with high contrast & size.
*   **Wall-of-text body copy with no paragraph breaks**
    *   Mistake: Wall-of-text body copy with no paragraph breaks
    *   Correction: Max 3 lines/paragraph on mobile, use white space.
*   **No alt text on images (blocked images = empty squares)**
    *   Mistake: No alt text on images (blocked images = empty squares)
    *   Correction: Every image gets descriptive alt text for context.
*   **Skipping the test send - publishing without pre-viewing**
    *   Mistake: Skipping the test send - publishing without pre-viewing
    *   Correction: Always send test email. Check on phone and laptop.

The image provides a clear and concise guide to common email design mistakes and how to correct them, making it a valuable resource for marketers and designers looking to improve their email campaigns.

The image presents a screenshot of an online course platform, specifically a lesson page for "Lesson 6.5: Building Email Campaigns in GHL." The platform's dark green background features white text and accents of orange and light green.

* **Module 6 Timeline**
	+ A vertical timeline on the left side displays the progression through Module 6.
	+ The timeline consists of five lessons:
		- Fundamentals (completed)
		- Strategy & Copy (completed)
		- Design Principles (completed)
		- GHL Implementation (in progress)
		- Launch & Scale (not started)
	+ A progress bar indicates that Lesson 4 of 10 is complete.
* **Lesson 6.5: Building Email Campaigns in GHL**
	+ The main section on the right side introduces Lesson 6.5.
	+ The lesson title is prominently displayed in large white text.
	+ A brief description explains that this hands-on session focuses on building sequences, configuring automations, and publishing the first campaign from scratch using GoHighLevel.
	+ A green button invites users to continue to Lesson 6.5.
* **Additional Elements**
	+ An orange button with white text reads "COMING UP NEXT."
	+ A logo in the top-right corner features the word "Libre" in light green text.
	+ A small icon resembling a laptop with code on its screen is situated above the lesson title.

In summary, the image showcases a lesson page for an online course, specifically Lesson 6.5, which focuses on building email campaigns in GoHighLevel. The page provides a clear overview of the lesson's objectives and invites users to proceed with the lesson.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to designing effective emails, titled "Design Serves Copy - Not the Other Way Around." The title is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by the subtitle "The hierarchy every email must follow to convert."

**Hierarchy Pyramid:**

*   A green pyramid on the left side of the image illustrates the hierarchy of elements in an email:
    *   **COPY:** The words that persuade - subject line, body, CTA
    *   **DESIGN:** The structure that makes copy readable and credible. Design makes copy easy to read - it does not replace it.
    *   **DELIVERY INFRASTRUCTURE:** Platform, automation, sending domain. The foundation - invisible when working correctly.

**Design Competing with Copy vs. Design Serving Copy:**

*   A table on the right side of the image compares two design approaches:
    *   **DESIGN COMPETING WITH COPY:**
        *   Decorative images that distract from the headline
        *   Multiple font sizes and colors creating visual noise
        *   CTA button buried in design elements
        *   Heavy graphics taking focus away from message
        *   Result: Reader admires the design - then closes without clicking
    *   **DESIGN SERVING COPY:**
        *   Clean layout that guides the eye to the headline first
        *   Consistent, simple typography that aids scanning
        *   CTA button visually dominant and isolated
        *   Design choices that build credibility and trust
        *   Result: Reader absorbs the message - then clicks

**Key Takeaway:**

*   A black box at the bottom of the image emphasizes the importance of prioritizing copy over design:
    *   When someone reads your email, they should remember what you said - not how it looked. Memorable design in email is invisible design.

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise guide to designing effective emails, highlighting the importance of prioritizing copy and using design to support it. By following this hierarchy and design approach, email designers can create emails that convert and engage their audience.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to email layout fundamentals, providing six essential rules for creating effective and high-converting emails. The title "Email Layout Fundamentals" is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a subtitle that reads, "The structural rules that every high-converting email follows." The infographic is divided into two sections: a central graphic illustrating an email template and six numbered rules surrounding it.

**Email Template Graphic:**
The central graphic depicts a sample email template with a white background, featuring a green button at the bottom. The template is accompanied by six numbered rules, each with a brief description:

**Rules:**

* **1. Single Column Layout:** Use one column for all marketing emails - multi-column layouts break on mobile and create reading confusion.
* **2. Width: 600px Maximum:** 600px is the industry standard - wider emails get cropped or require horizontal scrolling on most email clients.
* **3. Headline First - Always:** The first thing the reader sees must be the headline from your copy template - not a logo or banner image.
* **4. Font Size: 14-16px Min:** Smaller text forces readers to zoom on mobile. Use 14px minimum for body text, 18-22px for headlines.
* **5. White Space:** Padding between elements (min 16-24px) makes emails scannable and signals quality. Cramped layouts feel untrustworthy.
* **6. Footer is Non-Negotiable:** Every email must include: physical mailing address, unsubscribe link, and business name - these are legal requirements.

**Additional Information:**
At the bottom of the image, a dark blue box contains additional information in white and green text:

* Single column, 600px wide, Headline first, Readable font sizes, Generous white space, Legal footer.
* These six rules cover 90 percent of layout decisions you will ever face in email design.

**Logo and Branding:**
In the top-right corner, the word "Libre" appears in green text, accompanied by a green bar above it. The background of the image features a white grid pattern, providing a clean and organized visual representation of the email layout fundamentals.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to establishing brand consistency in email marketing, emphasizing the importance of creating instantly recognizable emails. The title, "Brand Consistency: Making Every Email Instantly Recognizable," sets the tone for the presentation.

* **Logo Usage**
	+ Place logo in the header - top center or top left
	+ Keep it consistent - same version in every email
	+ Maximum height: 100 to 120 pixels
	+ Always link the logo to the client's website
* **Typography**
	+ Max 2 font families: Headline (display) & Body
	+ Web-safe body fonts: Arial, Georgia, Verdana
	+ Custom fonts as images for headlines only
	+ Clear Hierarchy: Headline > Subhead > Body
* **Color Palette**
	+ One primary brand color for CTA buttons/accents
	+ Never use more than 3 colors in a single email
	+ High contrast: Dark text on light background
	+ Avoid red text - often flagged by spam filters
* **Tone & Voice**
	+ Consistency builds expectations and trust
	+ Define 3 tone descriptors (e.g., Direct, Warm)
	+ Check every email against your descriptors
	+ Inconsistent voice confuses readers

The infographic effectively conveys the key elements of brand consistency in email marketing, providing actionable tips and statistics to help businesses create a cohesive and recognizable brand identity across all touchpoints. By following these guidelines, companies can establish trust with their audience and increase the effectiveness of their email campaigns.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to mobile-first email design, emphasizing the importance of designing for smaller screens first. The title "Mobile-First Email Design" is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a subtitle that highlights the significance of mobile devices in email communication.

**Key Statistics:**

* Over 60% of emails are opened on mobile devices.

**Visual Representation:**

A large smartphone graphic dominates the left side of the image, showcasing an example email with various design elements labeled, including:

* Single Image (Full Width)
* Headline (22px Min)
* Short Paragraphs
* Full-Width Button
* Generous Padding

**Design Guidelines:**

The right side of the image features a series of guidelines for effective mobile-first email design, organized into eight sections:

* **Font Size:** Body text minimum 14px, headlines minimum 22px. Anything smaller forces zoom and readers bounce.
* **Line Length:** Maximum 60 characters per line. Longer lines require horizontal scanning causing readers to lose place.
* **CTA Button Width:** Use Full Width buttons. Narrow buttons require precise tapping; full width removes friction completely.
* **Single Images Only:** Never use side-by-side images. Multi-column layouts stack vertically and destroy hierarchy.
* **Tap Targets:** Any clickable element must be at least 44px tall. Body links are hard to tap; use buttons.
* **Preview Mobile:** Use GoHighLevel's mobile preview toggle for every single email before scheduling.

**Common Mistake:**

A red warning box at the bottom of the image cautions against designing for desktop first and then checking mobile, as this can lead to a broken experience for 60% of the audience.

**Branding:**

The Libre logo is displayed in the top-right corner, adding a touch of professionalism to the design.

Overall, the image provides a clear and concise guide to creating effective mobile-first email designs, highlighting key statistics, design guidelines, and best practices to ensure a seamless user experience.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to designing mobile-first email templates, emphasizing the importance of prioritizing mobile devices in the design process. The title "Mobile-First Email Design" is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a subtitle that highlights the significance of mobile devices in email communication.

**Key Statistics and Design Principles**

* Over 60% of emails are opened on mobile devices
* Design for smaller screens first

**Visual Representation**

A large smartphone graphic on the left side of the image illustrates an example of a well-designed email template, featuring:
* A single image (full width)
* Headline (22px min)
* Short paragraphs
* Full-width button
* Generous padding

**Design Guidelines**

Eight rectangular boxes on the right side of the image provide detailed design guidelines, each with:
* A green icon
* A brief description
* A relevant tip or best practice

The guidelines cover essential aspects of mobile-first email design, including:
* **Font Size**: Body text minimum 14px, headlines minimum 22px
* **Line Length**: Maximum 60 characters per line
* **CTA Button Width**: Use full-width buttons
* **Single Images Only**: Avoid side-by-side images
* **Tap Targets**: Clickable elements must be at least 44px tall
* **Preview Mobile**: Use GoHighLevel's mobile preview toggle

**Common Mistake and Warning**

A pink box at the bottom of the image warns against designing for desktop first and then checking mobile, highlighting the potential consequences of a broken mobile experience for 60% of the audience.

**Branding and Attribution**

The Libre logo is displayed in the top-right corner of the image, indicating the source of the information. Overall, the image provides a clear and concise guide to designing effective mobile-first email templates, emphasizing the importance of prioritizing mobile devices in the design process.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to effectively using images in emails, highlighting their potential benefits and drawbacks. The title, "Images in Email: What Works, What Hurts, What Gets You Flagged," sets the tone for the informative content that follows.

Here is a detailed breakdown of the image:

* **When Images Add Value**
	+ **Product Mockup**: A product mockup makes the intangible feel tangible, making it more relatable to the audience.
	+ **Author Photo**: Including an author photo humanizes the brand and helps build a connection with the sender.
	+ **Results/Proof**: Using results or proof, such as a dashboard screenshot or before-and-after comparison, makes claims more credible.
	+ **Branded Dividers**: Simple graphic dividers between sections improve scannability without posing a high load risk.
* **Technical Rules for Email Images**
	+ **Text-to-Image Ratio: 60/40**: The minimum text-to-image ratio should be 60% text to 40% images to avoid triggering spam filters.
	+ **File Size: <200KB**: Compressing all images to a file size under 200KB ensures fast loading times and prevents frustration.
	+ **Alt Text Mandatory**: Providing alt text for images is essential for accessibility and ensures that readers can understand the content even if images are blocked.
	+ **Width: 600px Max**: The maximum width for images should be 600px to prevent horizontal scrolling and ensure a smooth viewing experience.
	+ **Hosted Images Only**: Hosting images only via URL (e.g., GHL handles this automatically) ensures that images are easily accessible and load quickly.
	+ **No Image-Only Emails**: Emails with no readable text are likely to be flagged as spam and are inaccessible to screen readers.
* **Images That Hurt Deliverability**
	+ **All-Image Emails**: Emails consisting entirely of images have no deliverability, as spam filters require text to evaluate content.
	+ **Oversized Images**: Images above 1MB significantly increase load time and spam risk, making compression essential.
	+ **Embedded Text**: Text embedded in images cannot be read by clients or filters, making it crucial to use real HTML text instead.

In summary, the image provides valuable insights into the effective use of images in emails, highlighting both the benefits and potential pitfalls. By following the technical rules and avoiding images that hurt deliverability, email senders can create engaging and accessible content that resonates with their audience.

The image presents a comprehensive pre-send design checklist for email marketing, comprising 25 essential items to ensure optimal email design and functionality. The checklist is organized into five categories: **Layout & Structure**, **Branding**, **Mobile Verification**, **Images**, and **Final Send Check**.

*   **Layout & Structure**
    *   Single column layout used throughout
    *   Email width is 600px or less
    *   Headline appears first — before any body text block
    *   Font size is 14px or larger for body text
    *   Font size is 18px or larger for headlines
    *   Adequate white space between all sections (16px+)
    *   Legal footer included (address, business name, unsubscribe)
*   **Branding**
    *   Logo present in header — consistent with previous emails
    *   Brand colors used consistently — no more than 3
    *   Font family consistent with brand guidelines
    *   Tone matches brand voice descriptors
*   **Mobile Verification**
    *   Mobile preview checked in GoHighLevel editor
    *   Headline readable at mobile zoom level
    *   CTA button is full width on mobile
    *   No side-by-side image columns on mobile
    *   All tap targets are at least 44px tall
    *   Body text maximum 3 lines per paragraph
*   **Images**
    *   Text-to-image ratio is at least 60% text
    *   All images are below 200kb
    *   All images have descriptive alt text
    *   No image-only sections — all images accompanied by text
    *   Hosted images only — no attachments
*   **Final Send Check**
    *   Sent test email to personal inbox (desktop + mobile)
    *   All links are clickable and go to correct destinations
    *   Unsubscribe link functions correctly

The checklist provides a thorough guide for ensuring that emails are well-designed, functional, and effective across various devices and platforms. By following this checklist, marketers can significantly improve the quality and impact of their email campaigns.

The image presents a concise and informative guide to common email design mistakes that can undermine effective copy. The title, "7 Email Design Mistakes That Undermine Great Copy," sets the tone for the list of pitfalls to avoid.

**Mistakes to Avoid:**

*   **Using a multi-column layout that breaks on mobile**
    *   Single column always — for every marketing email.
*   **Leading with large decorative banner image not headline**
    *   Headline appears first in body — first text must be headline.
*   **Using too many fonts or font sizes (3+ creates visual noise)**
    *   Two font families maximum, 2-3 size variations total.
*   **CTA button same visual weight as surrounding elements**
    *   CTA must be visually dominant with high contrast & size.
*   **Wall-of-text body copy with no paragraph breaks**
    *   Max 3 lines/paragraph on mobile, use white space.
*   **No alt text on images (blocked images = empty squares)**
    *   Every image gets descriptive alt text for context.
*   **Skipping the test send — publishing without pre-viewing**
    *   Always send test email. Check on phone and laptop.

**Key Takeaway:**

*   Fixing mistakes 1, 2, and 7 alone — single column, headline first, always test — eliminates the most common design failures in GoHighLevel email campaigns.

In summary, the image provides a clear and actionable guide to improving email design by avoiding common mistakes that can detract from the effectiveness of the copy. By following these guidelines, designers can create more engaging and effective emails that resonate with their audience.
